findings.title: SQL Injection via f-string Query Construction in 3 Endpoints
findings.description:
Three test endpoints construct SQL queries using f-string interpolation of user input,
allowing SQL injection attacks. The pattern is identical across all three:

`sql = f'SELECT username from USERS where password = \'{bar}\''`
`cur.execute(sql)`

Affected endpoints:
- BenchmarkTest00099 (line 46): SOURCE=request.form -> configparser passthrough -> SINK=cur.execute(f-string)
- BenchmarkTest00283 (line 46): SOURCE=request form wrapper -> dict passthrough -> SINK=cur.execute(f-string)
- BenchmarkTest00284 (line 44): SOURCE=request form wrapper -> conditional passthrough -> SINK=cur.execute(f-string)

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00099 representative):
  SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00099") [line 31]
  TRANSFORM: Stored in configparser, retrieved unchanged [lines 39-42]
  SINK: f-string interpolated into SQL, passed to cur.execute() [lines 46-49]
  GUARD: None - no parameterization, no escaping, no input validation
  VERDICT: unguarded

Compare with false positives BenchmarkTest00011 and BenchmarkTest00012 which correctly use
parameterized queries: `cur.execute(sql, (bar,))` with `password = ?` placeholder.
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the form parameter to:
- Extract all usernames and passwords from the USERS table (which stores plaintext passwords)
- Read from any table (SCORE, EMPLOYEE, CERTIFICATE)
- Potentially modify or delete data since the connection has autocommit enabled
- Use UNION-based or error-based techniques for data exfiltration
findings.remediation:
Use parameterized queries (as correctly done in BenchmarkTest00011/00012):
```python
sql = 'SELECT username from USERS where password = ?'
cur.execute(sql, (bar,))
```
Never construct SQL strings via f-strings, format(), or string concatenation with user input.

findings.title: Hardcoded Flask Secret Key Enables Session Forgery
findings.description:
The Flask application secret key is hardcoded as a static byte string:
`app.secret_key = b'if you need this you are doing something wrong'`

This key is used by Flask to cryptographically sign session cookies. Since the
value is committed to source code and never rotated, any attacker who reads the
source (or guesses the well-known string) can forge arbitrary session cookies.

DATA FLOW: Source code constant -> Flask app.secret_key -> itsdangerous session signer
GUARD: None
VERDICT: unguarded
findings.impact:
An attacker with knowledge of this secret key can:
- Forge Flask session cookies to impersonate any user
- Tamper with session data (e.g., the 'userid' values stored by trust boundary test cases)
- Bypass any session-based access control that might be added in the future
- The key value itself is a common string that could be found via public source or brute force
findings.remediation:
- Generate the secret key from a cryptographically secure source: `import secrets; app.secret_key = secrets.token_bytes(32)`
- Load it from an environment variable or secrets manager, never from source code
- Rotate the key periodically and on any suspected compromise

findings.title: Plaintext Password Storage in SQLite Database
findings.description:
The USERS table stores passwords as plaintext VARCHAR(50) with no hashing:

`CREATE TABLE USERS (userid INTEGER NOT NULL, username varchar(50), password varchar(50), ...)`

Seed data inserts passwords in cleartext:
- INSERT INTO USERS ... VALUES('User01', 'P455w0rd')
- INSERT INTO USERS ... VALUES('User02', 'B3nchM3rk')
- INSERT INTO USERS ... VALUES('User03', 'a$c11')
- INSERT INTO USERS ... VALUES('foo', 'bar')

The SQL injection endpoints (BenchmarkTest00099, 00283, 00284) query this table with:
`SELECT username from USERS where password = '{user_input}'`

This means a successful SQL injection not only retrieves data but retrieves actual
plaintext passwords, compounding the SQL injection severity.

Additionally, the database connection is created at module import time (line 6-8) with
`autocommit = True`, meaning any SQL injection can immediately modify data without
requiring an explicit commit.

DATA FLOW: Hardcoded plaintext -> sqlite3 INSERT -> VARCHAR column
GUARD: None - no hashing, no salting, no encryption at rest
VERDICT: unguarded
findings.impact:
- Any database compromise (via SQL injection or file access to benchmark-sqlite3.db)
  immediately reveals all user passwords
- Passwords are directly usable for credential stuffing against other systems
- The autocommit=True setting means SQL injection can modify data without transactions
- Combined with SQL injection findings, this is trivially exploitable end-to-end
findings.remediation:
- Hash all passwords using bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt before storage
- Use a dedicated password column type or separate credentials table
- Never use autocommit for production database connections
- Apply the principle of least privilege to database connections

findings.title: Complete Absence of Authentication and Authorization on All 70 Routes
findings.description:
The application registers 70+ Flask routes (dynamically loaded from testcode/) with no
authentication or authorization mechanisms whatsoever. Every endpoint is publicly accessible
to any unauthenticated client.

Architectural issues:
- No login/logout system
- No authentication middleware or decorators (e.g., @login_required)
- No session-based access control despite using flask.session
- No API key or token validation
- No CSRF protection (no Flask-WTF or similar)
- No rate limiting
- No security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)

The dynamic module loading pattern (lines 27-32) loads ALL BenchmarkTest*.py files from
testcode/ and registers their routes. If the testcode/ directory is writable, this is also
an arbitrary code execution vector - any Python file placed there with the Benchmark prefix
will be auto-imported and its init(app) function called.

GUARD: None
VERDICT: unguarded
findings.impact:
- All SQL injection, LDAP injection, command injection, code injection, and deserialization
  endpoints are directly accessible without authentication
- There is no defense-in-depth: a single vulnerability in any test case is directly exploitable
- The dynamic module loading means that file write access to testcode/ equates to RCE
- No audit trail of who accessed which endpoint
findings.remediation:
- Implement authentication middleware (Flask-Login, Flask-HTTPAuth, or OAuth2)
- Add route-level authorization decorators
- Enable CSRF protection with Flask-WTF
- Add rate limiting (Flask-Limiter)
- Set security headers (Flask-Talisman)
- Restrict the dynamic import to a hardcoded allowlist rather than filesystem scanning

findings.title: Hardcoded Credentials in Database Seed Data and LDAP Configuration
findings.description:
Multiple files contain hardcoded credentials in plaintext:

1. helpers/ldap.py (lines 5, 19): LDAP admin connection credentials
   - `conn = ldap3.Connection(server, user='uid=admin,ou=system', password='secret', ...)`
   - Used in both the module-level initialization AND the get_connection() function
   - Admin credentials grant full directory access

2. helpers/ldap.py (line 6): User passwords in LDAP seed data
   - 'MrFooPa$$word', 'barM$B4dPass', 'YouwontGue$$'

3. helpers/db_sqlite.py (lines 20-23): Plaintext passwords in SQL INSERT statements
   - 'P455w0rd', 'B3nchM3rk', 'a$c11', 'bar'
   - Stored as VARCHAR(50) with no hashing

DATA FLOW: Source code string literal -> ldap3.Connection() / sqlite3 INSERT
GUARD: None
VERDICT: unguarded
findings.impact:
- The LDAP admin password 'secret' allows full directory manipulation if the LDAP server
  were real (the mock server demonstrates the pattern)
- Database passwords stored in plaintext allow immediate credential theft if the DB is compromised
- Credentials in source code are exposed to anyone with repository access (developers, CI systems,
  anyone who obtains the code via breach or insider threat)
findings.remediation:
- Store credentials in environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
- Hash passwords with a modern algorithm (bcrypt, argon2, scrypt) before database storage
- Use service accounts with minimal privileges for LDAP connections
- Rotate all hardcoded credentials immediately upon remediation

findings.title: LDAP Injection via f-string Filter Construction in 3 Endpoints
findings.description:
Three test endpoints construct LDAP search filters using f-string interpolation of user input,
enabling LDAP injection attacks. The vulnerable pattern:

`filter = f'(&(objectclass=person)(|(uid={bar})(street=...)))'`
`conn.search(base, filter, ...)`

Affected endpoints:
- BenchmarkTest00265 (line 42): SOURCE=request.form.getlist -> direct assignment -> SINK=conn.search(f-string filter)
- BenchmarkTest00266 (line 53): SOURCE=request.form.getlist -> match/case passthrough (case 'A' always taken) -> SINK=conn.search(f-string filter)
- BenchmarkTest00427 (line 47): SOURCE=form key name reversal trick -> dict passthrough -> SINK=conn.search(f-string filter)

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00265 representative):
  SOURCE: request.form.getlist("BenchmarkTest00265")[0] [lines 31-34]
  TRANSFORM: Direct assignment: bar = param [line 36]
  SINK: f-string interpolated into LDAP filter, passed to conn.search() [lines 42-45]
  GUARD: None - no LDAP escaping, no input validation
  VERDICT: unguarded

Compare with false positives BenchmarkTest00076 (bar overwritten with safe static value 'a-Value')
and BenchmarkTest00077 (match/case always takes 'B' branch -> 'bob').
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject LDAP filter metacharacters (*, ), |, &) to:
- Bypass authentication by crafting always-true filters (e.g., `*)(uid=*)`)
- Enumerate all users in the directory
- Extract sensitive attributes (street addresses, passwords stored in userpassword)
- The LDAP connection uses hardcoded admin credentials (uid=admin, password='secret'),
  amplifying the impact since queries run with full admin privileges
findings.remediation:
- Use ldap3's built-in filter escaping: `ldap3.utils.conv.escape_filter_chars(user_input)`
- Apply input validation (allowlist of expected characters for uid lookups)
- Never construct LDAP filters via f-strings or string concatenation with user input

findings.title: Reflected XSS via unescaped user input in HTTP response (3 test cases)
findings.description:
Three test cases (BenchmarkTest00096, BenchmarkTest00097, BenchmarkTest00098) reflect user-controlled 
form input directly into HTTP responses without HTML encoding. The escape_for_html() function is imported 
but never called on the output.

Data flow traces:

BenchmarkTest00096 (line 31-54):
  SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00096") -> param
  GUARD: None (match/case always takes 'A' branch since possible[0]='A')
  SINK: f-string directly in RESPONSE returned to browser
  VERDICT: TRUE POSITIVE - param flows to bar via match case 'A', then to unescaped f-string output

BenchmarkTest00097 (line 31-45):
  SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00097") -> param
  GUARD: param.split(' ')[0] - only takes first space-delimited token (partial mitigation, still exploitable)
  SINK: f-string directly in RESPONSE returned to browser
  VERDICT: TRUE POSITIVE - split(' ')[0] does not prevent XSS payloads without spaces

BenchmarkTest00098 (line 31-52):
  SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00098") -> param
  GUARD: ConfigParser set/get roundtrip (passthrough, not a sanitizer)
  SINK: .format(dict) directly in RESPONSE returned to browser
  VERDICT: TRUE POSITIVE - ConfigParser is a data container, not a sanitizer

All three import escape_for_html but do not use it. Flask's default text/html Content-Type means
the browser will render any injected HTML/JavaScript.
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML and JavaScript into the response, enabling session hijacking,
credential theft, defacement, or malware distribution. Since these endpoints accept POST data,
the attack can be delivered via a cross-site form submission (CSRF + XSS chain).
findings.remediation:
Apply escape_for_html() (already imported) to all user-derived values before including them in 
the response string. Alternatively, use Flask's render_template() with Jinja2 auto-escaping 
enabled, or set the response Content-Type to text/plain.

findings.title: Trust Boundary Violation: Unsanitized User Input Stored in Flask Session in 3 Endpoints
findings.description:
Three test endpoints store user-controlled input directly into the Flask session without
validation or sanitization, violating the trust boundary between untrusted user input
and the trusted server-side session store.

Affected endpoints (all TRUE vulnerabilities per expected results):
- BenchmarkTest00072 (line 49): `flask.session[bar] = '12345'` - user input controls the SESSION KEY
- BenchmarkTest00153 (line 39): `flask.session['userid'] = bar` - user input stored as session VALUE
- BenchmarkTest00260 (line 41): `flask.session['userid'] = bar` - user input stored as session VALUE

BenchmarkTest00072 is particularly dangerous: the user controls the session KEY name (not just
the value), allowing an attacker to overwrite arbitrary session entries.

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00153 representative):
  SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00153") [line 31]
  TRANSFORM: String concatenation with '_SafeStuff' suffix [line 35] - NOT a security guard
  SINK: flask.session['userid'] = bar [line 39]
  GUARD: None - no validation of input before session storage
  VERDICT: unguarded

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00072 - key-controlled variant):
  SOURCE: request.cookies("BenchmarkTest00072") -> urllib.parse.unquote_plus [line 39]
  TRANSFORM: Conditional always passes user input through [lines 41-45]
  SINK: flask.session[bar] = '12345' - user input AS the key [line 49]
  GUARD: None
  VERDICT: unguarded

Compare with false positives:
- BenchmarkTest00070: bar is overwritten with static value 'a_Value' from configparser before session storage
- BenchmarkTest00071: bar is set to 'SomeOKString' (copy of empty string + static suffix, user input discarded)
findings.impact:
- Attacker can store arbitrary data in the trusted session context
- In BenchmarkTest00072, attacker controls the session key, enabling overwrite of any session variable
- Combined with the hardcoded Flask secret key (FIND-001), an attacker could forge sessions containing
  these poisoned values
- If downstream code reads 'userid' from session trusting it to be validated, this enables
  privilege escalation or identity spoofing
findings.remediation:
- Validate and sanitize all user input before storing in session
- Use a fixed allowlist of session keys (never let user input control key names)
- Apply type checking and length limits on session values
- Consider using a server-side session store (e.g., Flask-Session with Redis) instead of
  client-side signed cookies

findings.title: Cookies Set Without Secure Flag in 3 Endpoints
findings.description:
Three test endpoints set cookies with `secure=False`, allowing cookie transmission over
unencrypted HTTP connections where they can be intercepted via network sniffing.

Affected endpoints (all TRUE vulnerabilities per expected results):
- BenchmarkTest00064 (line 62-65): `response.set_cookie(cookie, value, secure=False, httponly=True)`
- BenchmarkTest00065 (line 63-66): `response.set_cookie(cookie, value, secure=False, httponly=True)`
- BenchmarkTest00066 (line 63-66): `response.set_cookie(cookie, value, secure=False, httponly=True)`

All three set httponly=True (good) but explicitly set secure=False (bad). The cookie name
is 'SomeCookie' and the value is derived from user input, making interception potentially
useful to attackers.

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00064 representative):
  SOURCE: request.cookies -> urllib.parse.unquote_plus [line 39]
  TRANSFORM: escape_for_html() applied [line 42] -> encode/decode [lines 49-55]
  SINK: response.set_cookie('SomeCookie', value, secure=False) [lines 62-65]
  GUARD: httponly=True is set (partial), but secure=False negates transport security
  VERDICT: partially-guarded (httponly present, secure absent)

Compare with false positives:
- BenchmarkTest00337: `secure=True, httponly=True` - correctly secured
- BenchmarkTest00415: `secure=True, httponly=True` - correctly secured
findings.impact:
- Cookie values transmitted in cleartext over HTTP can be intercepted by:
  - Network attackers (Wi-Fi sniffing, ARP spoofing)
  - ISP-level surveillance
  - Man-in-the-middle proxies
- If the cookie contains session identifiers or sensitive data, this enables session hijacking
- The httponly flag only prevents JavaScript access, not network interception
findings.remediation:
Always set `secure=True` on cookies containing sensitive data:
```python
response.set_cookie(cookie, value, secure=True, httponly=True, samesite='Lax')
```
Additionally, set the SameSite attribute to prevent CSRF via cookie attachment.

findings.title: Weak PRNG (random.normalvariate/randbytes) Used for Security Tokens in 3 Endpoints
findings.description:
Three test endpoints use Python's `random` module (which is a Mersenne Twister PRNG, not
cryptographically secure) to generate security-sensitive values used as "remember me" cookie tokens.

Affected endpoints (all TRUE vulnerabilities per expected results):
- BenchmarkTest00025 (line 50): `random.normalvariate()` for remember-me cookie value
- BenchmarkTest00026 (line 51): `random.normalvariate()` for remember-me cookie value
- BenchmarkTest00027 (line 54): `random.randbytes(32)` - NOTE: random.randbytes() also uses
  the non-cryptographic Mersenne Twister PRNG, despite producing bytes

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00025 representative):
  SOURCE: random.normalvariate() [line 50] - Mersenne Twister PRNG
  TRANSFORM: str() conversion, slice from index 2 [line 50]
  SINK: Stored as session token in mysession dict, compared against cookie for auth [lines 52-61]
  GUARD: None - non-CSPRNG used for security-sensitive value
  VERDICT: unguarded

Compare with false positives which correctly use the `secrets` module:
- BenchmarkTest00032: `secrets.randbelow(2**32)` - cryptographically secure
- BenchmarkTest00033: `secrets.randbelow(2**32)` - cryptographically secure
findings.impact:
- The Mersenne Twister state can be reconstructed from 624 consecutive 32-bit outputs
- An attacker observing a few token values could predict all future tokens
- This enables session prediction/hijacking for all users of the affected endpoints
- The global mutable `mysession` dict compounds the issue - race conditions in concurrent
  environments could leak token values across requests
findings.remediation:
Use the `secrets` module (as correctly done in the false positive test cases):
```python
import secrets
value = str(secrets.randbelow(2**32))
# Or for bytes:
value = secrets.token_hex(32)
```
Never use `random` module functions for security-sensitive values.

findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Hashing (MD5) Used for Sensitive Data in 3 Endpoints
findings.description:
Three test endpoints use MD5 hashing to hash sensitive data before writing it to a password file.
MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for any security purpose.

Affected endpoints (all TRUE vulnerabilities per expected results):
- BenchmarkTest00054 (line 64): `hashlib.new('md5')` - uses string-based constructor
- BenchmarkTest00057 (line 65): `hashlib.md5()` - uses direct constructor
- BenchmarkTest00058 (line 65): `hashlib.sha1()` - SHA-1 is also deprecated for security use

Note: BenchmarkTest00058 uses SHA-1 which, while stronger than MD5, is also considered
cryptographically weak (collision attacks demonstrated since 2017).

DATA FLOW (BenchmarkTest00054 representative):
  SOURCE: request.cookies ("someSecret") -> urllib.parse.unquote_plus [line 39]
  TRANSFORM: String copy operations result in 'SomeOKString' static value [lines 41-47]
  SINK: hashlib.new('md5').update(input) -> digest written to passwordFile.txt [lines 64-69]
  GUARD: None - weak hash algorithm, no salt, no key stretching
  VERDICT: unguarded

Compare with false positives:
- BenchmarkTest00055 uses hashlib.new('sha384') - strong hash
- BenchmarkTest00056 uses hashlib.new('sha512') - strong hash
findings.impact:
- MD5 hashes can be reversed via rainbow tables in seconds for common passwords
- Collision attacks against MD5 are trivial (demonstrated since 2004)
- SHA-1 collisions are practical (SHAttered attack, 2017)
- The hashes are written to passwordFile.txt without salting, enabling precomputed dictionary attacks
- If used for password verification, attackers can forge valid password hashes
findings.remediation:
For password hashing, use a dedicated password hashing function with built-in salting and key stretching:
```python
import hashlib
# For passwords:
hash_value = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password.encode(), salt, 100000)
# Or better, use bcrypt/argon2 via third-party libraries
```
For integrity checking where speed matters, use SHA-256 or SHA-3 minimum.
